summary:
The Kanem Empire (c. 700–1380 AD) was one of the great states of the central Sahel region, centered around Lake Chad in what is today northeastern Nigeria, Chad, and Niger. Founded by the Duguwa dynasty and later ruled by the Saifawa dynasty — which claimed descent from the legendary Yemeni hero Sayf ibn Dhi Yazan — Kanem grew wealthy by controlling the trans-Saharan trade routes that connected sub-Saharan Africa with North Africa and the Mediterranean world. The primary exports were slaves, ivory, ostrich feathers, and animal hides; imports included horses, salt, copper, and North African manufactured goods.

Kanem's conversion to Islam — beginning gradually from the 9th century and becoming official under the mai (king) Humay around 1085 AD — transformed the kingdom into one of the major Islamic states of sub-Saharan Africa. The Saifawa dynasty's embrace of Islam brought literacy in Arabic, Islamic legal institutions, and connections with the broader Muslim world, including correspondence with North African sultans and Abbasid caliphs. Kanem eventually lost control of its core territory around Lake Chad due to internal conflicts and the pressure of the Bulala people in the late 14th century, with the ruling dynasty relocating westward to establish the Bornu Empire — which continued Kanem's political traditions and lasted until the 19th century, making the Saifawa dynasty one of the longest-ruling in world history.
